title: Cleft Lip and Palate: Comprehensive Management
objectives: Understand the embryology of facial development and cleft formation
Classify clefts according to anatomical involvement

sections.title: Epidemiology
sections.content:
**Most common craniofacial anomaly**

• Cleft lip ± palate: 1/700 births
• Isolated cleft palate: 1/2,500 births
• Male predominance for CL±P
• Female predominance for isolated CP

**Risk Factors:**
• Genetic factors (multifactorial)
• Maternal smoking, alcohol
• Anticonvulsants (phenytoin, valproate)
• Folate deficiency
• Family history (30-40x increased risk with affected sibling)

sections.title: Embryology
sections.content:
**Normal Facial Development (Weeks 4-10):**

**Primary Palate (Lip + Anterior Palate):**
• Fusion of medial nasal prominences
• Completed by week 6
• Failure → Cleft lip

**Secondary Palate (Hard + Soft Palate):**
• Fusion of palatine shelves
• Completed by weeks 7-10
• Failure → Cleft palate

**Key Concept:** Lip and palate form separately - can have isolated defects or both

sections.title: Classification
sections.content:
**Cleft Lip:**
• Unilateral vs bilateral
• Complete (to nostril floor) vs incomplete
• Simonart's band (tissue bridge)

**Cleft Palate:**
• Primary palate (anterior to incisive foramen)
• Secondary palate (posterior to incisive foramen)
• Complete vs incomplete
• Submucous cleft (occult - bifid uvula, zona pellucida)

**Combined:**
• Unilateral complete cleft lip and palate (most common)
• Bilateral complete cleft lip and palate

sections.title: Associated Problems
sections.content:
**Feeding:**
• Cannot generate negative pressure
• Nasal regurgitation
• Special bottles/nipples required

**Hearing:**
• Eustachian tube dysfunction
• Chronic otitis media with effusion
• PE tubes often needed

**Speech:**
• Velopharyngeal insufficiency
• Hypernasal speech
• Articulation errors

**Dental:**
• Missing/malformed teeth
• Malocclusion
• Alveolar bone grafting needed

**Psychosocial:**
• Appearance concerns
• Bullying
• Support essential

sections.title: Multidisciplinary Care Timeline
sections.content:
**Rule of 10s for Lip Repair:**
• 10 weeks old
• 10 pounds weight
• Hemoglobin 10 g/dL

**Surgical Timeline:**
• 3-6 months: Lip repair
• 9-12 months: Palate repair
• 8-11 years: Alveolar bone graft
• Adolescence: Rhinoplasty, orthognathic surgery

**Team Members:**
• Plastic/craniofacial surgery
• ENT
• Speech pathology
• Audiology
• Orthodontics
• Genetics
• Social work/psychology

sections.title: Syndromes & Sequences
sections.content:
**Pierre Robin Sequence:**
• Micrognathia → Glossoptosis → Airway obstruction + Cleft palate

**Van der Woude Syndrome:**
• Cleft lip/palate + lip pits
• Autosomal dominant (IRF6)

**Velocardiofacial (22q11):**
• Cleft palate, cardiac defects, immune deficiency
• CATCH-22

**Stickler Syndrome:**
• Cleft palate, myopia, joint problems
• Collagen disorder

**When to Suspect Syndrome:**
• Isolated cleft palate (30% syndromic)
• Other anomalies
• Family history
• Genetics referral recommended

keyTakeaways: Cleft lip and palate form from different embryologic processes
Isolated cleft palate has higher rate of syndromic association
Feeding is the immediate concern - special techniques required
Rule of 10s guides timing of lip repair
Hearing and speech require ongoing monitoring

reviewQuestions.question: A newborn is noted to have a bifid uvula and a palpable notch in the hard palate, but mucosa appears intact. What is this condition?
reviewQuestions.explanation: Submucous cleft palate is characterized by the classic triad: bifid uvula, zona pellucida (thin midline mucosa), and notched hard palate. It can cause velopharyngeal insufficiency and requires monitoring for speech development.
